content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5601        322-5601        322-5441        322-6441

July 23, 1979

Peter A. Bagatelos
Dobbs and Nielsen
Suite 2500, The Alcoa Building
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, CA 94111

Dear Mr. Bagatelos:

This is in response to your letter of June 27, 1979.
Instead of confirming your written version of my telephone
advice of June 26, 1979, I am responding as if your June 27
letter were a request for advice. I do this in order to
fully set out the facts as I understand them and because the
staff, after discussing the issues you raised, disagreed
with the advice I gave you in one important respect. You
should also be aware that the immunity provided by Section
83114 will not attach to a person who chooses not to identify
himself. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18320(a)(2). Therefore,
because your client has chosen to remain unidentified, it
will not obtain the benefits of Section 83114 immunity.

The facts, as I understand them, are as follows: Your
client is a committee which received a contribution from
Wells Fargo Bank acting as trustee. The contribution contained a notation that the contribution was from a numbered
trust. The bank refused to provide the name of the trust to
the committee, claiming confidentiality.

In these circumstances, the committee has an obligation
to use due diligence to obtain information concerning the
contribution from the bank. The manner in which the contribution was made raises a question as to whether the bank was
acting as an intermediary for another person who retained
the authority to dictate the terms of contributions made out
of the numbered trust account. Because of the possibility
of the intermediary situation, it is incumbent upon the
committee to seek the information from the bank concerning
the contribution including the contributor's name. If the
bank is acting as an intermediary and reveals the contributor,
the committee must report the person actually making the

Peter A. Bagatelos
July 23, 1979
Page Two


contribution and also report the bank as an intermediary.
Government Code Section 84302. If the bank was acting as an
intermediary, it also has an obligation under Government
Code Section 94302 to inform the committee of the name of
- the actual contributor, and in making inquiries of the bank
the committee should make the bank aware of its obligation.

Although the most likely situation is that the bank is
acting as an intermediary, it is possible that the bank
itself is the contributor. This could occur if the bank as
trustee is given complete discretion with respect to the
amount and recipient of contributions and the trustor and
the beneficiaries played no role in the contribution deci-
sions. However, if the bank is the contributor it is likely
to be a recipient committee because the person or persons
contributing to the trust would have known or had reason to
know that their funds, or at least a part of them, would be
used by the bank to make contributions. See Government Code
section 82013; 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18213(c). As a
recipient committee the bank would be required to file a
statement of organization and file campaign statements re-
porting the source of their funds. See Government Code
Sections 84200-206, 84210, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c).

In the unlikely event that the bank informs the com-
mittee that it is the contributor and not acting as an inter-
mediary, the committee should report the bank as the contri-
butor unless it has other knowledge indicating that the bank
is not the contributor.

If the bank refuses to disclose its status or admits
that it is an intermediary without revealing the name of the
contributor, the contribution is anonymous because there is
no means to identify the donor. An anonymous contribution
of $100 or more must be given to the Secretary of State.
Government Code Section 84304. However, Commission policy
is that a contribution is not deemed to have been accepted
if it is not negotiated and returned to the donor before the
closing date of the statement on which it would otherwise
have been reported. Therefore, in circumstances such as
these where the immediate source of the money is known, it
would also be proper to return the check to the bank prior
to the closing date of the campaign statement for the period
in which the check is received.

Peter A. Bagatelos
July 23, 1979
Page Three


If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to call me.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Lee C. Rosenthal
Chief
Legal Division

LCR:kp

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940


June 27, 1979


Mr. Lee Rosenthal
Fair Political Practices Commission
100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Lee:

     This will confirm our telephone conversation
of June 26, 1979 regarding reporting requirements
related to contributions received from a trust.

     I explained that one of our clients had
received a contribution from a bank, acting as trustee,
with a notation that the contribution was from a
numbered trust. I indicated that, upon request by
our client for the name of the trust, the bank refused
to provide the information because of confidentiality.

     You indicated that the trustor should be
disclosed as the contributor, with an indication that
the bank is acting as intermediary, assuming that the
trustor exercises the decision making power to make
the contribution. In the event that the trustee bank
is given discretion as to how to distribute money
from the trust, you indicated that the bank could be
a recipient committee if it receives and controls
$500 or more.

     You advised that our client should use reasonable
diligence to obtain the name of the trust from the
bank for disclosure purposes. In the event that best
efforts by our client to obtain the information fail,
you indicated that the client could retain the contribution and disclose such information as is available
to it at the time its report must be filed. With
respect to whether such a contribution is anonymous,
you indicated your feeling that it is not anonymous
since there is a name, i.e., the name of the bank,
associated with the contribution and tracing of the
funds are possible because of the numbered description
of the trust account.


SACRAMENTO OFFICE • SUITE 250, 1225 EIGHTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 • (916) 446-6752

Mr. Lee Rosenthal
Fair Political Practices Commission
June 27, 1979
Page 2


     Our client, upon being advised of the foregoing,
indicated that they would follow this advice in pro-
cessing and reporting the contribution.

     Thank you for your assistance.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Peter A. Bagatelos

PAB:gdf

sections.question_synthetic: What are the reporting requirements for a political committee that receives a contribution from a bank acting as trustee of a numbered trust when the bank refuses to disclose the trust's name?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The committee must exercise due diligence to obtain the contributor's identity from the bank, as the bank may be acting as an intermediary. If the bank refuses disclosure or cannot be identified as the actual contributor, the contribution is anonymous and must either be reported to the Secretary of State (if $100+) or returned to the bank before the statement closing date. The committee cannot simply retain the contribution without disclosure of the actual contributor's identity.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (dated June 27, 1979) and FPPC response letter (dated July 23, 1979). FPPC explicitly states it is responding to the written request rather than confirming phone advice, and notes the staff disagreed with prior telephone advice on one important point. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers, but content clearly structured as request followed by response. High confidence in extraction despite lack of formal section headers.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the reporting requirements for a political committee that receives a contribution from a bank acting as trustee of a numbered trust when the bank refuses to disclose the trust's name?

CONCLUSION: The committee must exercise due diligence to obtain the contributor's identity from the bank, as the bank may be acting as an intermediary. If the bank refuses disclosure or cannot be identified as the actual contributor, the contribution is anonymous and must either be reported to the Secretary of State (if $100+) or returned to the bank before the statement closing date. The committee cannot simply retain the contribution without disclosure of the actual contributor's identity.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides advice on reporting requirements for contributions from a bank trustee of a numbered trust. The FPPC corrects its earlier informal phone advice, clarifying that due diligence is required to identify the actual contributor, and that an unidentified contribution must be reported as anonymous or returned.
